metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the truncated `'esource'` grep pattern in AC3's proving command with the full phrase `'resource-server-only'` (or `'no AS'` / `'no DCR'`) to anchor the check on the specific design decision the ADR must record.
evidence: AC3 proving command: `for s in 'esource' 'composite' 'DNS-rebinding' ...`
Defect taxonomy #1: substring false-positive — 'esource' is an unanchored substring that matches trivially in any document discussing resource servers.

The ADR filename itself is 'mcp-optional-auth-resource-server.md' — if grep runs on the filename it would not apply here, but the document content will certainly contain 'resource' in the first paragraph, making this anchor vacuous.
findings.finding: AC3's proving command uses the truncated pattern 'esource' (with grep -Fi) as the anchor for the resource-server-only decision. This pattern matches any word containing the substring 'esource' (e.g. 'resource', 'Resource', 'resources') and would trivially pass if the ADR filename or a boilerplate sentence mentions 'resource' even once — it does not anchor on the specific claim 'resource-server-only'.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 proving command (grep pattern 'esource')
scenarios: An ADR that mentions 'resource' in its title/first sentence but omits the resource-server-only design decision passes the 'esource' check trivially.
The truncated pattern appears to be an attempt to match both 'resource' and 'Resource' case-insensitively, but -Fi already handles case; the truncation adds no value and weakens the anchor.
findings.suggested_fix: Change `'esource'` to `'resource-server'` in the AC3 for-loop; since `grep -Fi` is already used, case sensitivity is handled and the full substring 'resource-server' will match 'resource-server-only' as required by the ADR content spec.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the 'out of scope' grep anchor in AC1's proving command with a phrase that actually appears in the required residual-risks content, e.g. `'per-tool'` or `'rate-limit'` or `'≥128-bit'` (at least one phrase mandated by the What section's residual-risks bullet).
evidence: Plan What section: 'Residual risks (documented, not fixed here): authenticated ≠ authorized…per-tool scope→capability authorization is deferred; rate-limiting/brute-force is delegated…'
AC1 proving command: `grep -Fq "out of scope" docs/mcp-auth.md || exit 1` (the 'out of scope' entry in the for-loop)
Defect taxonomy #4: fixture invalidity — the anchor does not actually exercise the claim it purports to prove.
findings.finding: AC1's proving command uses the grep anchor 'out of scope' to verify coverage of the residual-risks section, but the plan's What section labels that content 'Residual risks (documented, not fixed here)' — an executor could write the required content without ever using the exact phrase 'out of scope', causing the proving command to fail despite correct content; or could write 'out of scope' superficially without covering the three required sub-topics (per-tool authz deferral, rate-limiting delegation, static-token entropy), causing the command to pass with incomplete content.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1 proving command (grep anchor 'out of scope')
scenarios: Executor writes a '## Residual Risks' section with all required content but never uses the phrase 'out of scope' → AC1 fails spuriously.
Executor adds a single sentence containing 'out of scope' in an unrelated context → AC1 passes without the required sub-topics being present.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `'out of scope'` in the AC1 for-loop with `'per-tool'` (which must appear when documenting 'per-tool scope→capability authorization is deferred') and add `'rate-limit'` as a second anchor; both are directly mandated by the What section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC3's proving command to also grep for at least the advisory ID strings cited in the What section (e.g., add `'GHSA-9h52'`, `'CVE-2026-40575'`, `'CVE-2026-27124'`, `'PVE-2026-93393'` to the loop) so the criterion verifies what the body describes.
evidence: What: 'The advisory identifiers (GHSA-9h52-p55h-vw2f, CVE-2026-40575, CVE-2026-27124, PVE-2026-93393) are cited as upstream-tracked references for those classes'
AC3 proving command: `for s in 'esource' 'composite' 'DNS-rebinding' 'forwarded-header' 'confused-deputy' 'audience' 'defer'` — no advisory ID strings included
findings.finding: The What section explicitly requires the ADR to cite specific advisory identifiers (GHSA-9h52-p55h-vw2f, CVE-2026-40575, CVE-2026-27124, PVE-2026-93393) as upstream references, but the ADR AC's proving command checks only for general vulnerability class keywords ('DNS-rebinding', 'forwarded-header', 'confused-deputy', 'audience') — the specific advisory IDs are not verified by any criterion.
findings.location: What section (ADR bullet, advisory IDs paragraph) vs AC3 proving command
scenarios: Implementer writes the ADR with only vulnerability class headings and no advisory IDs; AC3 passes but the explicitly described citation requirement from the What section is unmet, leaving the ADR's audit trail incomplete.
findings.suggested_fix: Append the advisory IDs to the AC3 loop: `for s in 'esource' 'composite' 'DNS-rebinding' 'forwarded-header' 'confused-deputy' 'audience' 'defer' 'GHSA-9h52' 'CVE-2026-40575' 'CVE-2026-27124' 'PVE-2026-93393'; do grep -Fiq "$s" docs/adr/0050-mcp-optional-auth-resource-server.md || exit 1; done`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align the AC1 proving-command grep string with the What section's language: either change `'out of scope'` to `'deferred'` (matching the What section's residual-risks phrasing) or add 'out of scope' explicitly to the What section's required content.
evidence: AC1 for-loop includes: `'out of scope'` as the final required string
What section uses: 'per-tool scope→capability authorization is deferred', 'rate-limiting/brute-force is delegated to the edge proxy in v1'
The word 'deferred' appears three times in What; 'out of scope' does not appear in What
findings.finding: AC1's shell proving command tests for the literal string `'out of scope'` but the What section's residual-risks content plan exclusively uses 'deferred' language ('per-tool scope→capability authorization is deferred', 'rate-limiting/brute-force is delegated'). An author writing faithfully to the What spec would not naturally produce the string 'out of scope', causing the gate to fail even when the document is complete and correct.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1 proving command (`grep -Fq 'out of scope'`) vs. What section ('Residual risks')
scenarios: An implementer writes the residual-risks section using 'deferred items' / 'future work' headings faithful to the What spec; the CI gate rejects an otherwise-complete doc because the exact string 'out of scope' is absent.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `'out of scope'` with `'deferred'` in the AC1 for-loop, since the What section already uses 'deferred' three times for the residual-risks content.

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).

verify command `' 'single audience' 'Behind a proxy' 'interactive-client' 'out of scope'; do grep -Fq "$h" docs/mcp-auth.md || exit 1; done` has an unquoted shell variable ($VAR/$?) in its grep — it expands before grep runs; the pattern is not what you wrote

verify command `test -f docs/adr/0050-mcp-optional-auth-resource-server.md && for s in 'esource' 'composite' 'DNS-rebinding' 'forwarded-header' 'confused-deputy' 'audience' 'defer'; do grep -Fiq "$s" docs/adr/0050-mcp-optional-auth-resource-server.md || exit 1; done` has an unquoted shell variable ($VAR/$?) in its grep — it expands before grep runs; the pattern is not what you wrote
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add 'update `docs/README.md` to link `mcp-auth.md`' as an explicit bullet in the What section so the deliverable is described in the body, not only implied by AC2.
evidence: What section lists: 'New docs/mcp-auth.md', 'A new ADR under docs/adr/', 'Final reference/manifest consistency check' — docs/README.md update is absent.
AC2: '`grep -Fq 'mcp-auth.md' docs/README.md && python scripts/check_docs_index.py --check`'
findings.finding: The 'What' section does not describe updating docs/README.md as a deliverable, but AC2's proving command requires `grep -Fq 'mcp-auth.md' docs/README.md && python scripts/check_docs_index.py --check` — an implementer reading only the 'What' section would miss this required file change.
findings.location: What section (three deliverables listed) vs AC2 proving command
scenarios: Implementer writes all three described deliverables, runs AC2's proving command, and it fails because docs/README.md was never updated — the deliverable was only specified in the AC, not the body.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a fourth bullet to the What section: '- Update `docs/README.md` to link `docs/mcp-auth.md` (required by the docs-index completeness gate).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add 'update `docs/README.md` to link `mcp-auth.md`' explicitly to the What section and the Scope line so the deliverable is not invisible to the implementer.
evidence: What section lists: 'docs/mcp-auth.md', 'A new ADR under docs/adr/', 'Final reference/manifest consistency check' — no mention of editing docs/README.md.
Scope section: 'docs/mcp-auth.md, the ADR, and the final reference/manifest consistency verification only.'
AC2 proving command: 'grep -Fq 'mcp-auth.md' docs/README.md && python scripts/check_docs_index.py --check'
grep of docs/README.md for 'mcp-auth' returns no matches — the link does not yet exist.
check_docs_index.py find_unindexed() reports any top-level *.md not linked from the index, so mcp-auth.md will also fail the --check gate unless it is linked.
findings.finding: The 'What' section (Scope) enumerates exactly three deliverables — docs/mcp-auth.md, the ADR, and the reference/manifest consistency check — but omits updating docs/README.md, even though AC2's proving command hard-requires `grep -Fq 'mcp-auth.md' docs/README.md`. The docs/README.md currently does not link mcp-auth.md (confirmed by directory read and search), so this edit is real work. A scope description that doesn't name it means an implementer following the 'What' section alone will miss it and AC2 will fail.
scenarios: An implementer reads the What/Scope sections and creates docs/mcp-auth.md and the ADR but does not touch docs/README.md. AC2's grep exits 1 and the check_docs_index.py --check gate also exits 1, failing the story.
findings.suggested_fix: In the ## What section, after the docs/mcp-auth.md bullet, add: 'Link `docs/mcp-auth.md` from `docs/README.md` under the Operator section.' In ## Scope, change the first sentence to: '`docs/mcp-auth.md` (plus its `docs/README.md` entry), the ADR, and the final reference/manifest consistency verification only.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `docs/README.md` to the 'What' section's deliverable list and to `file_impact` as a required edit (insert a link to `mcp-auth.md` in the Operator section), since AC2's proving command requires both `grep -Fq 'mcp-auth.md' docs/README.md` and `python scripts/check_docs_index.py --check` to exit 0.
evidence: AC2 proving command: `grep -Fq 'mcp-auth.md' docs/README.md && python scripts/check_docs_index.py --check`

check_docs_index.py lines 43-60: the checker scans docs/README.md for links and flags any top-level *.md not linked from it — so docs/mcp-auth.md (a new top-level file) will be flagged UNLESS docs/README.md is updated with a link.
The plan 'What' section lists only: docs/mcp-auth.md, the ADR, and the reference/manifest consistency check — no mention of editing docs/README.md.
file_impact lists: docs/mcp-auth.md, docs/adr/, docs/mcp-reference.md, docs/env-vars.md, server.json — docs/README.md is absent.
findings.finding: `docs/README.md` must be edited to add a link to `mcp-auth.md` for AC2's proving command to pass, but it is absent from the plan's 'What' section, 'Scope' section, and `file_impact` list.
findings.location: What / Scope / file_impact / AC2 proving command
scenarios: An implementer following the plan's 'What' and 'Scope' creates the new docs/mcp-auth.md but does not update docs/README.md; AC2's grep exits 1, the check_docs_index.py gate also fails — the AC is unpassable.
A reviewer reading file_impact does not flag a missing README.md edit, leaving the CI docs-index gate broken.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section, add a bullet: 'Edit `docs/README.md` (Operator section) to link `mcp-auth.md`.' Add `{"path": "docs/README.md", "reason": "add mcp-auth.md link to satisfy AC2 docs-index gate"}` to file_impact.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify each of the four advisory identifiers (GHSA-9h52-p55h-vw2f, CVE-2026-40575, CVE-2026-27124, PVE-2026-93393) resolves to a real, publicly accessible advisory record matching the vulnerability class attributed to it in the ADR; if any are fictional stand-ins or mismatched, replace with accurate identifiers or remove and describe the class directly.
evidence: Plan text: 'The advisory identifiers (GHSA-9h52-p55h-vw2f, CVE-2026-40575, CVE-2026-27124, PVE-2026-93393) are cited as upstream-tracked references for those classes, NOT asserted as independently NVD-verified facts.'
No match for any of these identifiers in any repo file other than plan-review cache entries.
PVE namespace (Python Vulnerability Exchange / PyPA) does not typically cover MCP protocol-level audience-confusion issues — the namespace mismatch warrants verification.

ADR 0046 (docs/adr/0046-security-posture-and-accepted-limitations.md line 183) cites CVE-2026-44544 in a verifiable context (gittuf rollback), demonstrating the existing pattern for real advisory citations.
findings.finding: The ADR (AC3) is required to cite four specific advisory identifiers — GHSA-9h52-p55h-vw2f, CVE-2026-40575, CVE-2026-27124, PVE-2026-93393 — as 'upstream-tracked references.' PVE-2026-93393 uses the Python Vulnerability Exchange (PyPA advisory) namespace, which is an unusual namespace for an MCP audience-confusion issue; none of the four identifiers appear anywhere in the codebase or in the existing ADR corpus (0046-security-posture covers CVE-2026-44544 via a real NVD link). If any of these IDs are fictional or mismatched to the described vulnerability class, the ADR will mislead future security reviewers who attempt to look up the upstream tracking.
findings.location: What section — ADR bullet; AC3 proving command
scenarios: A security auditor looks up PVE-2026-93393 on the OSV/PyPA database and finds no record, concluding the ADR fabricated its rationale.
A future contributor searching for audience-confusion mitigations follows the ADR citation and finds a different vulnerability class, leading to a miscalibrated threat model.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a note to the ADR content requirement (or the 'What' section) that the implementer must verify each advisory identifier resolves to its claimed vulnerability class before committing the ADR, or provide an explicit fallback (e.g., 'cite by class name with a placeholder if the ID cannot be confirmed').
evidence: Plan text: 'the transport-hardening vulnerability CLASSES it mitigates (DNS-rebinding, forwarded-header spoofing, confused-deputy, audience-confusion)... The advisory identifiers (GHSA-9h52-p55h-vw2f, CVE-2026-40575, CVE-2026-27124, PVE-2026-93393) are cited as upstream-tracked references for those classes, NOT asserted as independently NVD-verified facts.'
All four identifiers carry a 2026 year, placing them beyond training-data verification cutoff — Evidence class: Unverified.
The AC3 proving command does not grep for these identifiers directly, but the 'What' section requires them in the ADR text, making the implementer responsible for their accuracy.
findings.finding: The four security advisory identifiers cited in the ADR requirement (GHSA-9h52-p55h-vw2f, CVE-2026-40575, CVE-2026-27124, PVE-2026-93393) are all 2026-dated and cannot be confirmed to exist in any public advisory database from available evidence. While the plan hedges them correctly as 'upstream-tracked references for those classes, NOT asserted as independently NVD-verified facts', the AC3 proving command's grep for these exact strings (implicitly, they must appear in the ADR to satisfy the intent) means an implementer writing the ADR must include identifiers that are Unverified. If any of these IDs turns out to be fabricated or misattributed, the ADR would contain a misleading citation that survives the grep gate.
findings.location: AC3 — ADR content requirement; 'What' section — 'citing GHSA-9h52-p55h-vw2f, oauth2-proxy CVE-2026-40575, CVE-2026-27124 confused-deputy, PVE-2026-93393'
scenarios: An implementer copies the four identifiers verbatim into the ADR. One or more resolves to a different vulnerability than claimed, or does not exist in NVD/GitHub advisory DB. The ADR ships with a misleading citation that passes all grep-based gates.
A security reviewer independently validates the ADR and finds a misattributed CVE, requiring a follow-up correction commit.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Require `docs/mcp-auth.md` to explicitly instruct operators, in the `static` verifier flat-config example, to supply the token value via an environment variable or a gitignored secrets file — not as a literal value in a committed config — and cross-reference the existing `docs/config.md` secrets-vs-settings policy.
evidence: Plan text: 'each of the five verifier modes (`static`, `jwt`, `introspection`, `proxy`, `custom`) with a flat-config example each'
Plan text: 'tokens/secrets never logged' — covers logging but not at-rest storage in committed config

docs/config.md §5: 'Secrets live only in the environment / a gitignored .env, never the committed config' — this policy exists but the plan does not require the operator guide to cross-reference or restate it for the static verifier example

ABSENCE: no requirement in the plan for the flat-config example to use an env-var placeholder (e.g. `token = '${REBAR_MCP_STATIC_TOKEN}'`) or to include a warning against committing the literal token value
findings.finding: The operator guide spec requires a 'flat-config example' for the `static` verifier mode, mandates ≥128-bit token entropy, and says 'tokens/secrets never logged' — but does not require the guide to explicitly instruct operators to supply the static token value via environment variable or a gitignored file rather than embedding it in a committed config file. A naive operator following only the flat-config example could commit the live bearer token to their repository.
findings.location: What section — operator guide bullet: 'flat-config example each' for the static verifier mode; also 'tokens/secrets never logged' bullet
scenarios: Operator follows the flat-config example literally, pastes the generated static token into rebar.toml, and commits it — the bearer credential is now in git history and reachable by anyone with repo read access.
Operator serving rebar-mcp on a public endpoint uses a committed static token; git history exposure gives an attacker full write access to the rebar store.
findings.suggested_fix: In the AC1 proving-command string list (or in the What-section guide spec), add a required phrase such as `'never commit the token'` or `'supply via environment variable'` that the grep oracle must find in the static-verifier section, analogous to how 'tokens/secrets never logged' is already a required phrase. Example addition to the flat-config example requirement: 'the `static` example MUST show the token value supplied via an env-var reference or note that the value must not appear in committed config.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `scripts/check_docs_index.py` pre-exists in the repo (cite the commit/PR that introduced it) or add its creation to this story's scope, and verify the `--check` flag name matches the actual script interface.
evidence: AC2 text: 'python scripts/check_docs_index.py --check (the docs-index completeness gate exits 0 with the new file linked)'
No plan section creates or references the creation of this script.
Defect taxonomy #7: missing prerequisite-state — the command assumes state a prior step must establish, but no prior step is named.
findings.finding: AC2's proving command invokes `python scripts/check_docs_index.py --check`, but the plan does not confirm this script pre-exists in the repository, nor does it appear in the scope of this story or any named S1–S6 story. If the script does not exist, the proving command exits non-zero regardless of whether the doc is correctly linked, blocking AC2 completion.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 proving command (`python scripts/check_docs_index.py --check`)
scenarios: The script does not exist in the repo → AC2 fails with FileNotFoundError regardless of doc content.
The script exists but uses a different flag name (e.g. --validate vs --check) → silent failure per defect taxonomy #3.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Scope or Approach section whether `scripts/check_docs_index.py` is an existing artifact (created in S1–S6 or pre-existing) or must be created in S7, and if the latter, add it to the What deliverables list.
evidence: AC2: `grep -Fq 'mcp-auth.md' docs/README.md && python scripts/check_docs_index.py --check`
Scope section: 'docs/mcp-auth.md, the ADR, and the final reference/manifest consistency verification only. No code behavior.'
No mention of `check_docs_index.py` in What, Approach, or Scope sections
findings.finding: `scripts/check_docs_index.py --check` is invoked in AC2's proving command but the plan never establishes that this script already exists (from a prior story) or that S7 is responsible for creating it. If the script does not exist, AC2 always fails regardless of doc quality.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 proving command (`python scripts/check_docs_index.py --check`)
scenarios: The script does not exist in the repo; AC2 fails with a FileNotFoundError on every CI run, blocking merge even though the doc is correctly linked.
A new contributor cannot tell whether the script is inherited from S1–S6 or needs to be created in S7.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a 'Troubleshooting / Common errors' section to the `docs/mcp-auth.md` plan coverage list, specifying at minimum: the HTTP response shape on token rejection (status, WWW-Authenticate header content), startup validation errors for `auth_resource_server_url` (http vs https for non-loopback), and a diagnostic checklist for failed interactive-client OAuth flows; extend the AC grep to require a 'Troubleshooting' or 'Common errors' marker.
evidence: AC grep checks for: 'Enabling the HTTP transport', verifier mode headers, 'single audience', 'Behind a proxy', 'interactive-client', 'out of scope' — no troubleshooting or error-response section required
Plan lists 'residual risks (documented, not fixed here)' covering architectural deferrals, but not operator-facing error diagnosis
ABSENCE: no mention of what HTTP response an operator sees on token rejection, how to diagnose audience mismatch, or what a misconfigured `auth_resource_server_url` produces at runtime
findings.finding: The operator guide plan (docs/mcp-auth.md) specifies coverage of configuration happy paths and residual architectural risks, but does not specify a troubleshooting or error-diagnosis section covering what operators observe when misconfiguration occurs — e.g., what HTTP status/body is returned on token rejection, how to diagnose a wrong `auth_resource_server_url`, or what a failed `--check` script output looks like. The AC grep strings do not include any troubleshooting/error-response marker.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — first AC bullet (docs/mcp-auth.md content coverage) and the What section describing docs/mcp-auth.md
scenarios: Operator sets `auth_resource_server_url` to an http:// URL for a non-loopback bind and gets a startup error — guide doesn't say what to look for
Token is rejected due to audience mismatch — operator sees a 401 but the guide gives no diagnostic path
Interactive client OAuth flow fails because the IdP's DCR endpoint returns 403 — operator has no documented fallback checklist
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section under docs/mcp-auth.md, add a bullet: '**Troubleshooting:** HTTP 401 response shape and WWW-Authenticate header on token rejection; startup-time validation errors for `auth_resource_server_url` (non-loopback requires https); diagnosing failed interactive-client OAuth (DCR 403 → fall back to pre-registered client-id; audience mismatch → check `auth_resource_server_url` matches the token `aud`/`resource`).' Add `'Troubleshooting'` to the AC grep string set.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit, resolvable `parent_id` (the epic ticket ID) to the S7 plan so the G7 leaf-parent containment check can be executed against the parent's What/Scope/ACs.
evidence: Plan Scope: 'Subset of the epic. Depends on S1–S6 (documents them).' — names an epic but gives no ticket ID.
No `parent_id` field appears anywhere in the plan text.

All plausible parent ticket IDs tried via show_ticket returned 'Ticket not found': mcp-auth, epic-mcp-auth, mcp-auth-epic, mcp-http-auth, mcp-optional-auth, mcp-rs-auth, mcp-resource-server, mcp-auth-rs, MCP-AUTH, http-transport-auth, mcp-transport-auth, mcp-auth-0050, 0050-mcp-auth, mcp-auth-epic-2026, optional-auth, mcp-auth-S7, auth-rs-epic, mcp-rs, rs-auth.

G7 rubric: 'FETCH THE PARENT. The parent's id (parent_id) is provided in the ticket-graph context. Call show_ticket(<parent_id>) to read the parent's plan.' — precondition not satisfiable from this plan.
findings.finding: The leaf plan (S7) declares itself 'Subset of the epic. Depends on S1–S6 (documents them).' but provides no resolvable parent ticket ID, making the G7 leaf-parent containment check impossible to execute. The parent epic's What/Scope/ACs — the authoritative contract against which S7's deliverables (docs/mcp-auth.md, ADR 0050, reference drift gates, docs-index CI gate, coordinating S1–S6 output) must be verified as a subset — cannot be fetched.
findings.location: Scope section — 'Subset of the epic. Depends on S1–S6 (documents them).'
scenarios: S7 introduces a docs-index CI drift gate (check_docs_index.py --check wired into the proving command) that may not be present in the parent epic's scope — if the parent never authorized this gate, S7 is expanding scope.

S7 assigns itself a 'final consistency check coordinator' role over S1–S6 outputs (verifying the union of all new keys in mcp-reference.md and env-vars.md) — if the parent scoped S7 as a pure documentation leaf with no coordinating gate role, this is a scope divergence.

The ADR deliverable (docs/adr/0050-mcp-optional-auth-resource-server.md) with specific advisory citations (GHSA-9h52-p55h-vw2f, CVE-2026-40575, etc.) may or may not be inside the parent's contract; without the parent's plan it cannot be confirmed.
findings.suggested_fix: Embed the parent epic's ticket ID in the plan header (e.g., `parent_id: <epic-ticket-id>`) and call `show_ticket(<epic-ticket-id>)` to confirm S7's three deliverables (docs/mcp-auth.md, ADR 0050, reference/manifest drift verification) are all listed in the parent's What or Scope section without contradiction.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm and explicitly name in the ADR the specific MCP SDK class/interface (e.g., `OAuthAuthorizationServerProvider` in `mcp.server.auth.provider`) that each of the five verifier modes extends or implements, and note whether any mode requires going below that interface (e.g., ASGI middleware), so the 'official SDK seam' claim is verifiably accurate.
evidence: Plan text: 'building our own verifiers on the official SDK seam vs adopting standalone fastmcp'
OAuthAuthorizationServerProvider EXISTS in mcp.server.auth.provider (confirmed via resolve_symbol), but the plan does not name or verify the specific hook/interface used for each verifier mode.

The `proxy` verifier (forwarding token to an upstream endpoint) and `introspection` verifier (RFC 7662 token introspection) are non-trivial — whether they fit the SDK's provider interface without monkey-patching is material to the ADR's 'build vs adopt' justification.
mcp = ['mcp>=1.9'] in pyproject.toml — the SDK is pinned with only a floor, so the seam's stability across minor versions is also relevant.
findings.finding: The plan's core architectural justification for building custom verifiers states they build 'on the official SDK seam' (`mcp>=1.9`), but does not verify — or require S7 to document — which specific SDK extension point (interface, class, or hook) each of the five verifier modes (especially `proxy` and `introspection`) uses. `OAuthAuthorizationServerProvider` exists in `mcp.server.auth.provider`, but whether the `proxy` and `introspection` modes can be implemented through that interface without lower-level ASGI injection or SDK internals access is an unverified capability assertion that the ADR is supposed to record authoritatively.
findings.location: What section — ADR bullet ('building our own verifiers on the official SDK seam'); Approach & rationale
scenarios: The ADR asserts 'built on official SDK seam' but S1–S6 actually used private SDK internals or ASGI middleware injection; the ADR then mis-describes the architecture to future maintainers.
An SDK minor update (mcp 1.10+) changes the provider interface, breaking the verifiers in a way the ADR didn't anticipate because the seam was never precisely named.
findings.suggested_fix: In the ADR's 'build own verifiers' section, add a sentence like: 'Each verifier implements `OAuthAuthorizationServerProvider` from `mcp.server.auth.provider`; the `proxy` and `introspection` modes additionally require [X] because the provider interface does not expose [Y].' This makes the seam claim auditable.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
